## Key Concepts from Reinforcement Learning

This section identifies the reinforcement learning concepts most important for
interpreting the finance literature reviewed in this chapter. Formal definitions,
equations, and algorithm taxonomies are presented in Chapter 3; the focus here
is on why each concept is specifically consequential for trading problems.

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{tikz}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-cap: "The agent-environment interaction loop in reinforcement learning. At each step the agent observes state $S_t$ and reward $R_t$, selects action $A_t$, and receives the next state $S_{t+1}$ and reward $R_{t+1}$ from the environment."
\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Latex,
  thick,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners=5pt, minimum width=3.2cm, minimum height=1.1cm, font=\large}
]

\node[box] (agent) at (0, 3)  {Agent};
\node[box] (env)   at (0, 0)  {Environment};

% Right path (action)
\draw[->] (agent.east) -- ++(2.8,0) -- ++(0,-3) -- (env.east);
\node[right, align=left, font=\normalsize] at (2.8, 1.5) {action\\[2pt]$A_t$};

% Left path (state + reward)
\draw[->] (env.west) -- ++(-2.8,0) -- ++(0,3) -- (agent.west);
\node[left,  align=right, font=\normalsize] at (-2.8, 3)   {state\\[2pt]$S_t$};
\node[right, align=left,  font=\normalsize] at (-2.8, 1.5) {reward\\[2pt]$R_t$};

% Dashed line with next-step labels
\draw[dashed, thin] (-1.0, 0.55) -- (-1.0, -0.55);
\node[right, font=\small] at (-1.0, 0.45) {$R_{t+1}$};
\node[right, font=\small] at (-1.0, 0.15) {$S_{t+1}$};

\end{tikzpicture}
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
![The agent-environment interaction loop in reinforcement learning. At each step the agent observes state $S_t$ and reward $R_t$, selects action $A_t$, and receives the next state $S_{t+1}$ and reward $R_{t+1}$ from the environment.](diagrams/rl-loop.svg){fig-align="center"}
:::

Portfolio decisions are sequential and path-dependent. This makes the MDP
framework [@Bellman1957; @Puterman1994] the formal language for trading
agents. However, financial environments only approximately satisfy standard MDP
assumptions. Market regime shifts, structural breaks, and partial observability
all contribute to this approximation.

Bellman's principle of optimality motivates value-based reasoning
about long-run consequences of trading actions. But direct dynamic programming is
inapplicable because market transition dynamics are unknown and non-stationary.
This points toward model-free learning that estimates value directly from sampled
experience [@Sutton1988]. Temporal difference methods are especially relevant in finance. Q-learning in particular
[@Watkins1989] has an off-policy nature. This permits learning from historical trajectories
rather than requiring live interaction with the market.

Portfolio problems are naturally continuous. This makes tabular and discrete-action
value methods structurally inadequate. Policy gradient methods
[@Williams1992; @Sutton2000] and the actor-critic architectures they
support are the class for continuous position sizing. The
high-dimensionality of financial state representations makes function
approximation necessary. Deep RL methods [@Mnih2015] provide the required
generalization capacity. However, this comes at the cost of stability issues
in off-policy settings [@Tsitsiklis1997].

This fragility motivated the stabilization mechanisms in TD3 [@Fujimoto2018].
It also recurs throughout the applied trading literature.

Four practical complications distinguish financial RL from standard control
benchmarks. The exploration-exploitation tradeoff is more costly in trading than
in most settings. Exploratory actions incur immediate transaction costs,
while under-exploration prevents discovery of profitable policies.

This tension is amplified by non-stationarity. Strategies that exploit historical
regularities can decay abruptly after regime changes. Yet excessive exploration
destroys performance through churn and slippage [@Sutton2018; @Moody1998]. Reward
specification is a central design decision because it determines what the agent
actually optimizes. The reward-shaping literature [@Ng1999] is directly relevant
to finance. Misspecified rewards lead to metric gaming or tail-risk neglect.

Sharpe-style risk-adjusted objectives provide an alternative. The Differential
Sharpe Ratio [@Moody1998] aligns optimization with economically meaningful return
rather than with noisy single-step price changes. Sample efficiency is unusually important
because historical market data is finite. Live experimentation is also constrained
by capital and risk limits. Off-policy replay-based methods [@Lin1992] are
therefore methodological enablers rather than engineering conveniences.
They underpin the algorithm selection in this thesis. Non-stationarity is the dominant
reason why classical RL guarantees do not transfer cleanly to financial markets:
market dynamics, volatility regimes, and participant behavior evolve continuously,
which motivates the emphasis on robust feature engineering,
conservative optimization, and strict temporal validation over rolling
out-of-sample periods [@Deng2016].

Accordingly, claims that RL "works" in trading are inherently context-dependent: RL methods can produce useful policies when the learning
objective, state representation, and validation design are aligned with market
frictions and regime uncertainty.
